network.overall_strategy: This network is centered on the only experimentally verified prebiotic corridor to methionine identified in the literature: aldehyde generation, acrolein formation by aldol condensation of formaldehyde and acetaldehyde, Michael addition of methanethiol to acrolein to give methional, Strecker conversion of methional to the corresponding aminonitrile, and hydrolysis to methionine. Ten distinct pathways are provided as distinct precursor-sourcing and environment-coupling routes that all converge on the same literature-supported core target-forming sequence rather than inventing unsupported alternative terminal chemistries. Hydrothermal chemistry primarily supplies methanethiol under FeS- or MoS2-mediated conditions and can also contribute aldehyde precursors; surface spark-discharge/photochemical and evaporitic chemistry supplies HCN, NH3, formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, and acrolein; biochemical/prebiotic stages complete hydrolysis and network integration. The network explicitly addresses the key bottleneck from the literature: methanethiol availability.

reactions.name: Spark-discharge formation of hydrogen cyanide

conditions.other: electric discharge / volcanic lightning analogue in reducing gas mixture

reactions.mechanism: High-energy discharge fragments methane and ammonia to radicals that recombine to nitrile-bearing products including HCN.
reactions.reasoning: HCN is a standard Miller-type discharge product and a required Strecker reagent for methionine formation. The deep research report identifies spark discharge in CH4/NH3-containing systems as the relevant source environment for methionine chemistry. Surface volcanic lightning is favored geologically for methionine synthesis (Van Trump & Miller, 1972; Parker et al., 2011; Johnson et al., 2008). Mineral agents are included to satisfy schema requirements, but the key activation is electrical discharge.

reactions.name: Spark-discharge and photochemical formation of formaldehyde

conditions.other: UV irradiation and/or electric discharge in volcanic plume condensates

reactions.mechanism: Methane-derived radicals and partial oxidation/hydration chemistry generate methanal, which is then trapped in aqueous condensates.
reactions.reasoning: Formaldehyde is identified in the report as an abundant spark-discharge or photochemical aldehyde precursor feeding acrolein formation. Its availability in prebiotic atmospheric chemistry is consistent with Miller-type systems and later surface concentration chemistry (Van Trump & Miller, 1972; Cleaves, 2003; Johnson et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Spark-discharge formation of acetaldehyde

conditions.other: electric discharge / volcanic lightning followed by aqueous trapping

reactions.mechanism: Energetic gas-phase radical chemistry from methane-containing atmospheres yields low-molecular-weight aldehydes including ethanal.
reactions.reasoning: The report states that acrolein forms from formaldehyde and acetaldehyde and that both aldehydes are abundant products of spark discharge or photochemistry. This reaction supplies the validated acrolein precursor rather than inventing a direct route to acrolein (Van Trump & Miller, 1972; Cleaves, 2003; Parker et al., 2011).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal FeS-mediated methanethiol synthesis from CO2, H2S, and H2

conditions.other: FeS/H2S system under anoxic conditions; methanethiol low yield (~0.06% based on H2S)

reactions.mechanism: Iron sulfide surfaces mediate reduction of CO2 in the presence of H2 and H2S to methylated sulfur species, producing methanethiol.
reactions.reasoning: Methanethiol availability is the central bottleneck in prebiotic methionine synthesis. Heinen & Lauwers demonstrated FeS-mediated abiotic formation of methanethiol from CO2/H2S under anaerobic aqueous conditions at >=75C. This hydrothermal route is included as a literature-grounded upstream source feeding the only proven methionine corridor (Heinen & Lauwers, 1996; Reeves et al., 2014).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal supercritical CO2 sulfur reduction to methanethiol

conditions.catalyst: MoS2-like sulfide catalytic regime represented within Fe-S hydrothermal network
conditions.other: 14 days; highest reported abiotic CH3SH yield up to 7.9% conversion of H2S

reactions.mechanism: Sulfide-catalyzed reduction/methylation chemistry in supercritical CO2 produces methanethiol from inorganic carbon and sulfur feedstocks.
reactions.reasoning: The report identifies a 2024 study achieving the highest abiotic methanethiol yield in supercritical CO2 with MoS2 at 200C over 14 days. Because the schema requires hydrothermal mineral agents from the prescribed list, the reaction is framed within iron-sulfur hydrothermal context while explicitly noting that the literature catalyst is MoS2-like sulfide chemistry. This reaction is retained because the report highlights it as a major advance easing the methanethiol bottleneck (2024 Communications Earth & Environment study cited in report; Reeves et al., 2014).

reactions.name: Spark-discharge radical recombination to methanethiol

conditions.other: electric discharge / volcanic lightning; radical recombination of CH3• and HS•

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge generates methyl and hydrosulfide radicals that recombine to form methanethiol directly in the gas phase.
reactions.reasoning: The report explicitly states that spark discharge and volcanic lightning can directly generate methanethiol from CH4 and H2S through radical recombination, and that this is the route operative in Miller-type methionine-producing experiments (Van Trump & Miller, 1972; Parker et al., 2011).

reactions.name: Acrolein formation by aldol-condensation corridor from formaldehyde and acetaldehyde

conditions.other: very dilute aldehydes; neutral to basic aqueous conditions; evaporitic concentration can assist

reactions.mechanism: Aldol addition followed by dehydration converts formaldehyde/acetaldehyde-derived carbonyl chemistry into the α,β-unsaturated aldehyde acrolein.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction follows the specific validated acrolein route emphasized in the report and corrects prior oversimplification. Cleaves demonstrated acrolein formation from very dilute aldehyde solutions under neutral to basic aqueous conditions, establishing prebiotic plausibility. The report identifies acrolein as the pivotal branching intermediate for methionine chemistry (Cleaves, 2003; Van Trump & Miller, 1972).

reactions.name: Michael addition of methanethiol to acrolein forming methional

conditions.other: mild aqueous conditions; thiolate conjugate addition

reactions.mechanism: Methanethiol, via its thiolate form, undergoes 1,4-conjugate addition to acrolein to yield 3-methylthiopropionaldehyde (methional), creating the thioether bond of methionine.
reactions.reasoning: The report states that all experimentally verified prebiotic methionine syntheses converge on this Michael addition step, which forms the characteristic thioether bond and yields methional as the direct Strecker precursor (Van Trump & Miller, 1972; Parker et al., 2011).

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of methionine aminonitrile from methional

conditions.other: aqueous Strecker conditions in anoxic setting

reactions.mechanism: Methional forms an imine/iminium with ammonia that is trapped by cyanide to produce the corresponding α-aminonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the key Strecker step in the only proven methionine route. The report specifically identifies reaction of methional with HCN and ammonia to form an α-aminonitrile intermediate prior to hydrolysis. Industrial racemic methionine synthesis follows the same logic, and Van Trump & Miller established it as prebiotically robust (Van Trump & Miller, 1972; Parker et al., 2011).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of methionine aminonitrile to methionine

conditions.temperature: variable, mild to moderately heated aqueous cycling
conditions.other: prolonged hydrolysis during pool cycling or post-synthesis aqueous aging

reactions.mechanism: The aminonitrile is hydrolyzed through amide-like intermediates to the α-amino acid methionine.
reactions.reasoning: The report identifies hydrolysis of the methionine α-aminonitrile as the final step to methionine in the verified Strecker route. A separate biochemical/prebiotic hydrolysis node is used to connect surface synthesis to final amino acid accumulation without inventing a direct hydrothermal one-step methionine synthesis (Van Trump & Miller, 1972; Parker et al., 2011).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal aldehyde generation via Fischer-Tropsch-type carbon reduction

conditions.other: Fischer-Tropsch-type / CO2 hydrogenation regime producing low molecular weight oxygenates

reactions.mechanism: Metal oxide/sulfide surfaces reduce inorganic carbon to small oxygenated organics, including aldehyde precursors that can be transferred to surface settings.
reactions.reasoning: The report emphasizes formaldehyde and acetaldehyde as acrolein precursors and allows hydrothermal/surface interconnection. This reaction is included conservatively as an upstream source of aldehydes through well-known magnetite-catalyzed carbon reduction chemistry, without claiming a direct hydrothermal methionine route. It supports environmental coupling while preserving the literature-mandated core acrolein-methional-Strecker path (Huber & Wächtershäuser, 1997; Amend & Shock, 1998; report summary on FTT-like aldehyde availability).

pathways.name: Volcanic lightning complete surface route
pathways.description: All key precursors are generated in a volcanic lightning setting, then acrolein-methional-Strecker chemistry proceeds in surface condensates.

pathways.name: FeS-hydrothermal methanethiol feeding surface Strecker route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal FeS chemistry supplies methanethiol, while surface discharge supplies HCN and aldehydes for the validated methionine corridor.

pathways.name: Supercritical-CO2 methanethiol route to surface methionine
pathways.description: High-yield abiotic methanethiol from hydrothermal supercritical CO2 systems is coupled to surface acrolein and Strecker chemistry.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal aldehydes plus hydrothermal methanethiol route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal carbon reduction provides aldehyde feedstock and FeS hydrothermal chemistry provides methanethiol; acrolein and Strecker steps occur after transfer to cooler surface waters.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal aldehydes plus supercritical methanethiol route
pathways.description: Aldehydes from hydrothermal FTT-like chemistry and methanethiol from supercritical sulfide catalysis converge in surface acrolein/methional chemistry.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal aldehydes with lightning methanethiol route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal sources contribute formaldehyde and acetaldehyde, while volcanic lightning directly produces methanethiol and HCN.

pathways.name: Surface aldehydes with FeS methanethiol route
pathways.description: Spark/photochemical formaldehyde and acetaldehyde generation converges with hydrothermal FeS-derived methanethiol at acrolein and methional.

pathways.name: Surface aldehydes with supercritical methanethiol route
pathways.description: Surface photochemical/discharge aldehydes converge with hydrothermal supercritical-CO2 methanethiol production before the core Strecker sequence.

pathways.name: Miller-1958 style sulfur-rich discharge route
pathways.description: A sulfur-rich CH4/CO2/H2S/NH3 discharge environment generates the key feedstocks documented in archival Miller samples, followed by the established acrolein-methional-Strecker corridor.

pathways.name: Mixed hydrothermal-surface systems chemistry route
pathways.description: A maximum-interconnection path in which hydrothermal aldehydes and methanethiol are supplemented by surface HCN generation before surface acrolein, methional, aminonitrile, and final hydrolysis.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the strongest literature-aligned pathway. It matches the experimentally verified spark-discharge corridor to methionine: generation of HCN, formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, and methanethiol in sulfur-rich discharge chemistry, followed by acrolein formation, Michael addition to methional, Strecker synthesis, and hydrolysis. The main weakness is that acetaldehyde formation directly from CH4 + H2O is simplified and likely represents a discharge product ensemble rather than a clean stoichiometric transformation. Still, as a network-level route it is coherent and strongly supported by Van Trump and Miller 1972 and Parker et al. 2011.
suggestions: Specify a sulfur-rich spark discharge atmosphere closer to the Miller 1958 composition for rxn_001-rxn_003 and rxn_006.
Represent rxn_003 as discharge/photochemical product formation from a gas mixture rather than direct binary conversion.
Include explicit mild aqueous/basic conditions for rxn_007 and rxn_008.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: A strong mixed-environment route that sensibly uses hydrothermal FeS-mediated methanethiol production to address the key bottleneck while leaving the experimentally demonstrated terminal chemistry intact. This aligns well with Heinen and Lauwers 1996 for CH3SH sourcing and with the Van Trump and Miller corridor for methionine formation. Concern remains that hydrothermal methanethiol concentrations were likely low in natural systems, so the environmental transfer to surface concentration settings should be made explicit.
suggestions: Add concentration/transport justification for methanethiol moving from hydrothermal to surface settings.
Constrain rxn_004 to moderate hydrothermal conditions rather than generic vents, given methionine thermal lability.
Clarify mineral identity as mackinawite/FeS if the network intends strict compliance with the required mineral set.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This route is well designed around the 2024 MoS2/supercritical CO2 methanethiol result and the validated methional-Strecker corridor. It directly addresses the major literature bottleneck, methanethiol availability, better than the FeS route. The main caveat is whether supercritical-CO2-derived methanethiol can realistically interface with surface aldehyde and cyanide chemistry before dilution or loss.
suggestions: Specify MoS2 and supercritical CO2 conditions explicitly for rxn_005.
Add a geochemical transfer mechanism from supercritical hydrothermal production to surface evaporitic or volcanic pools.
Clarify whether the same basin receives both discharge-derived aldehydes and hydrothermal sulfur volatiles.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway is chemically coherent but depends heavily on hydrothermal aldehyde generation via Fischer-Tropsch-type chemistry. While hydrothermal organics and small aldehydes are plausible, simultaneous clean production of both formaldehyde and acetaldehyde in a way that feeds acrolein chemistry is less directly supported than spark-discharge aldehydes. The rest of the sequence remains strong and literature-grounded.
suggestions: Downgrade claims around direct hydrothermal formaldehyde/acetaldehyde production or cite specific FTT literature.
Add a quenching/cooling step before acrolein chemistry to avoid thermal destruction.
State that aldehydes generated hydrothermally are transferred to cooler surface settings for condensation.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway improves methanethiol sourcing relative to P4 by using the stronger modern MoS2/supercritical CO2 result, but it still relies on the least secure part of the network: hydrothermal aldehyde supply. Because the experimentally established methionine corridor is preserved from acrolein onward, target achievement remains strong despite uncertainty in precursor sourcing.
suggestions: Provide stronger literature support for rxn_011 or replace it with surface aldehyde sourcing if strict experimental alignment is desired.
Clarify that methionine itself is not synthesized hydrothermally, only its precursors.
Limit hydrothermal temperatures to moderate regimes to remain consistent with methionine instability.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This mixed route is plausible at the network level but less compelling than the pure discharge route because it couples uncertain hydrothermal aldehyde production with a surface lightning methanethiol source that could already have generated aldehydes locally. The pathway works, but the environmental partitioning appears somewhat unnecessary and weakens coherence.
suggestions: Either make the route fully surface-based or provide a strong transport rationale for hydrothermal aldehydes.
Specify why hydrothermal aldehydes are preferred over directly discharge-generated aldehydes.
Document expected concentration mechanisms for acrolein-forming aldehydes after hydrothermal export.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is essentially the inverse of P2 and remains a strong route. Surface aldehyde and HCN generation are literature-supported, and hydrothermal FeS methanethiol production addresses the sulfur bottleneck. The route is realistic if methanethiol can be transported or trapped into surface evaporitic systems at sufficient concentrations.
suggestions: Add concentration mechanisms such as evaporitic pooling or adsorption to mineral surfaces.
Clarify that hydrothermal methanethiol production is moderate-temperature and not black-smoker style.
Tighten the stoichiometric description of acetaldehyde formation.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is one of the best mixed-environment pathways. Surface generation of HCN and aldehydes is well supported, and MoS2-mediated methanethiol synthesis is the strongest non-discharge solution to the core sulfur bottleneck. The final sequence is exactly the verified methionine corridor. Main remaining issue is geochemical integration of the precursor pools.
suggestions: Explicitly define supercritical CO2 and MoS2 conditions in the pathway metadata.
Add a basin-interface scenario linking hydrothermal sulfur chemistry to surface organic chemistry.
Indicate how methanethiol is retained against volatilization.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway is effectively the classic Miller sulfur-rich discharge route and is among the most experimentally defensible in the network. It directly reflects the 1958 archived experiment reanalyzed in 2011 and the 1972 acrolein/methional proposal. As with P1, the main issue is that some early discharge precursor reactions are idealized as direct conversions rather than atmospheric product networks.
suggestions: Model discharge chemistry as arising from a gas mixture rather than individual pairwise reactants.
Add explicit mention of H2S-rich volcanic lightning conditions.
Include product-mixture language for aldehyde formation.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This systems-chemistry route captures the intended environmental interconnection well, combining hydrothermal sulfur chemistry with surface nitrogen/carbon fixation and biochemical completion. However, it inherits the main weakness of the network: hydrothermal aldehyde generation is much less securely evidenced than discharge-derived aldehydes. It is conceptually strong but somewhat less experimentally anchored than the best pathways.
suggestions: Reduce reliance on rxn_011 or support it with stronger aldehyde-generation literature.
Add explicit compartmentalization and concentration steps to improve systems-chemistry plausibility.
Emphasize moderate-temperature hydrothermal settings only for precursor formation.

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a strong synthesis network that performs well on topology, target achievement, and literature alignment. Its greatest strength is that it does not invent unsupported terminal chemistries: all successful routes converge on the only experimentally validated prebiotic methionine corridor, acrolein to methional to methionine aminonitrile to methionine. That is highly consistent with the literature summary, especially Van Trump and Miller 1972, Cleaves 2003, and Parker et al. 2011. The network also correctly identifies methanethiol as the central bottleneck and provides three sourcing modes that match the literature: spark discharge, FeS-mediated hydrothermal synthesis, and MoS2/supercritical CO2 synthesis. Topologically the network is connected and dense, with real convergence around methanethiol, acrolein, methional, and the aminonitrile. Environmental interconnection is also good, especially in mixed hydrothermal-surface routes. Main weaknesses are: several precursor-generation reactions are written as overly simplified net conversions rather than realistic atmospheric or hydrothermal product ensembles; hydrothermal aldehyde generation (rxn_011) is the least secure reaction in the network; and the evaluation criteria required explicit mineral agents for all surface and hydrothermal reactions, which are only partially specified in the summarized network description. There is also a mild inconsistency with the deep research conclusion that volcanic lightning environments are generally more favorable than hydrothermal systems for methionine itself, though the network mostly uses hydrothermal settings only for precursor supply. Overall, this is a good-to-excellent network and would be close to approval, but falls short of the >=0.9 approval threshold mainly because of incomplete mineral specification and uncertainty around hydrothermal aldehyde sourcing.
